SNGL
avatar
Standard Library / Components
User profile image with fallback.
badge
Standard Library / Components
Small status label — a compact chip showing a status, count, or category tag.
button
Standard Library / Components
Clickable button that triggers an action.
card
Standard Library / Components
Styled container for grouping related content.
checkbox
Standard Library / Components
Boolean toggle rendered as a checkmark box.
chip
Standard Library / Components
Compact interactive tag.
datepicker
Standard Library / Components
Calendar-based date selection.
divider
Standard Library / Components
Visual separator line.
drawer
Standard Library / Components
Slide-out panel anchored to a side of the viewport.
errorBoundary
Standard Library / Components
Catches errors raised in children's event handlers and expressions.
hbox
Standard Library / Components
Horizontal box layout container.
image
Standard Library / Components
Displays an image from a URL or asset path.
input
Standard Library / Components
Single-line text input field.
link
Standard Library / Components
Navigational hyperlink.
menu
Standard Library / Components
Context or dropdown menu.
menubar
Standard Library / Components
Horizontal menu bar — typically at the top of a window on desktop.
modal
Standard Library / Components
Dialog overlay.
popover
Standard Library / Components
Floating content panel anchored to a trigger.
progress
Standard Library / Components
Determinate progress indicator.
radio
Standard Library / Components
Group of mutually exclusive options.
scroll
Standard Library / Components
Scrollable viewport that wraps a single child.
select
Standard Library / Components
Dropdown selection control.
slot
Standard Library / Components
Placeholder for projected content in custom component definitions.
spacer
Standard Library / Components
Flexible empty space.
spinner
Standard Library / Components
Indeterminate loading indicator.
splitview
Standard Library / Components
Resizable two-pane layout.
stack
Standard Library / Components
Layered container where children overlap in the order they are declared.
table
Standard Library / Components
Tabular data display.
tabs
Standard Library / Components
Tabbed navigation.
text
Standard Library / Components
Displays a text string.
textarea
Standard Library / Components
Multi-line text input field.
timer
Standard Library / Components
Periodic callback.
toggle
Standard Library / Components
On/off switch control.
toolbar
Standard Library / Components
Horizontal bar of action buttons.
tooltip
Standard Library / Components
Informational popup on hover or focus.
tree
Standard Library / Components
Hierarchical data display.
vbox
Standard Library / Components
Vertical box layout container.
Alert
Standard Library / Types
Receiver type for transient user notifications.
Alert.confirm
Standard Library / Types / Alert
Prompts the user to confirm or cancel.
Alert.error
Standard Library / Types / Alert
Shows an error alert.
Alert.info
Standard Library / Types / Alert
Shows an informational alert.
Alert.toast
Standard Library / Types / Alert
Shows a short transient toast tagged with variant ("info", "success", "warning", "error") appropriate to the platform.
Alert.warn
Standard Library / Types / Alert
Shows a warning alert.
ChangeEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@change` when a control commits a new value — typically on blur, Enter, or an explicit selection change.
ClickEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@click` when a component is activated.
DateEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@change` from `datepicker` when the selected date changes.
DismissEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@close` for overlay components (modal, drawer, popover, chip remove).
ErrorEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@error` when an operation fails.
ExpandEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@expand` in hierarchical views such as `tree` when the user opens or collapses a node.
File
Standard Library / Types
Receiver type for file-system interactions.
File.pick
Standard Library / Types / File
Opens a file-picker dialog and returns the chosen path, or an empty string if cancelled.
File.pickFolder
Standard Library / Types / File
Opens a folder-picker dialog and returns the chosen path, or an empty string if cancelled.
FocusEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@focus` and `@blur` as focus moves in and out of a control.
InputEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@input` on every keystroke.
LoadEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@load` after an asynchronous resource (image, document) finishes loading.
LongPressEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@longPress` after a sustained press on pointer or touch platforms.
MenuEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@select` / `@close` on menu and menubar items.
RefreshEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@refresh` when a scrollable container requests pull-to-refresh.
ResizeEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@resize` when a split or resizable container changes geometry.
ScrollEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@scroll` / `@scrollEnd` as the viewport scrolls.
SelectEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@select` from list-like components (tabs, tree, table row selection).
Style
Standard Library / Types
Declarative style properties applied via the `style` prop on any visual component.
SubmitEvent
Standard Library / Types
Payload emitted by `@submit` on form-style input submission, typically triggered by pressing Enter inside a single-line input.
Test
Standard Library / Types
Receiver type for test-only helpers.
Test.assert
Standard Library / Types / Test
Asserts that b is true; fails the enclosing test otherwise.
Test.must
Standard Library / Types / Test
Like assert, but stops the test immediately when b is false.
color
Standard Library / Types
RGBA color with 8-bit channels.
color.darken
Standard Library / Types / color
Mixes c toward black by pct (0.0-1.0) on each RGB channel, preserving alpha.
color.hex
Standard Library / Types / color
Renders c as a `#RRGGBB` hex string.
color.lighten
Standard Library / Types / color
Mixes c toward white by pct (0.0-1.0) on each RGB channel, preserving alpha.
color.opacity
Standard Library / Types / color
Returns a copy of c with alpha replaced by a (0-255).
color.rgb
Standard Library / Types / color
Builds an opaque color from 0-255 RGB components.
color.rgba
Standard Library / Types / color
Builds a color from 0-255 RGB components and an explicit 0-255 alpha.
error
Standard Library / Types
Receiver type for raising runtime errors caught by an `errorBoundary` ancestor.
error.raise
Standard Library / Types / error
Raises a runtime error.
float
Standard Library / Types
64-bit floating-point number.
float.abs
Standard Library / Types / float
Returns the absolute value of a float.
float.acos
Standard Library / Types / float
Arccosine of x, result in radians within [0, π].
float.asin
Standard Library / Types / float
Arcsine of x, result in radians within [-π/2, π/2].
float.atan
Standard Library / Types / float
Arctangent of x, result in radians within [-π/2, π/2].
float.atan2
Standard Library / Types / float
Arctangent of y/x using signs of both arguments to pick the correct quadrant.
float.ceil
Standard Library / Types / float
Smallest integer value not less than x, as a float.
float.clamp
Standard Library / Types / float
Clamps x to the inclusive range [lo, hi].
float.cos
Standard Library / Types / float
Cosine of x, where x is in radians.
float.floor
Standard Library / Types / float
Largest integer value not greater than x, as a float.
float.max
Standard Library / Types / float
Returns the larger of two floats.
float.min
Standard Library / Types / float
Returns the smaller of two floats.
float.pow
Standard Library / Types / float
Raises base to the power exp.
float.round
Standard Library / Types / float
Nearest integer value to x, as a float.
float.sin
Standard Library / Types / float
Sine of x, where x is in radians.
float.sqrt
Standard Library / Types / float
Non-negative square root of x.
float.tan
Standard Library / Types / float
Tangent of x, where x is in radians.
int
Standard Library / Types
64-bit signed integer.
int.abs
Standard Library / Types / int
Returns the absolute value of an integer.
int.clamp
Standard Library / Types / int
Clamps x to the inclusive range [lo, hi].
int.max
Standard Library / Types / int
Returns the larger of two integers.
int.min
Standard Library / Types / int
Returns the smaller of two integers.
list
Standard Library / Types
Heterogeneous list.
list.contains
Standard Library / Types / list
True if item appears in l (by equality).
list.filter
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a new list containing the items of l for which pred returns true.
list.indexOf
Standard Library / Types / list
Index of the first occurrence of item in l (by equality), or -1 if absent.
list.join
Standard Library / Types / list
Joins the items of l into a single string, separated by sep.
list.length
Standard Library / Types / list
Number of items in l.
list.map
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a new list built by applying fn to each item of l.
list.push
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a new list with item appended to the end of l.
list.remove
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a new list with the item at index removed from l.
list.reverse
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a copy of l with items in reverse order.
list.slice
Standard Library / Types / list
Returns a new list containing items from start (inclusive) to end (exclusive).
string
Standard Library / Types
UTF-8 string.
string.contains
Standard Library / Types / string
True if sub appears anywhere in s.
string.endsWith
Standard Library / Types / string
True if s ends with suffix.
string.indexOf
Standard Library / Types / string
Index of the first occurrence of sub in s, or -1 if absent.
string.length
Standard Library / Types / string
Number of Unicode characters in s (not bytes).
string.lower
Standard Library / Types / string
Lowercased copy of s using the platform's Unicode case mapping.
string.replace
Standard Library / Types / string
Copy of s with every occurrence of old replaced by new.
string.split
Standard Library / Types / string
Splits s on every occurrence of sep and returns the pieces.
string.startsWith
Standard Library / Types / string
True if s begins with prefix.
string.substring
Standard Library / Types / string
Substring of s from start (inclusive) to end (exclusive).
string.trim
Standard Library / Types / string
Copy of s with leading and trailing whitespace removed.
string.upper
Standard Library / Types / string
Uppercased copy of s using the platform's Unicode case mapping.
Alignment
Standard Library / Enums
Cross-axis alignment of children inside a flex container.
BorderStyle
Standard Library / Enums
Line style drawn along a component's border.
Display
Standard Library / Enums
Intrinsic layout mode.
FlexDirection
Standard Library / Enums
Main-axis direction for flex containers such as `vbox` and `hbox`.
FontStyle
Standard Library / Enums
Slant applied to fonts.
FontWeight
Standard Library / Enums
Numeric weight for fonts.
Justification
Standard Library / Enums
Main-axis distribution of children inside a flex container.
ListStyle
Standard Library / Enums
Marker style for list-rendered containers.
ObjectFit
Standard Library / Enums
How an `image`'s contents are sized to fit its frame.
Overflow
Standard Library / Enums
Behavior when content exceeds its container's bounds.
Position
Standard Library / Enums
Positioning scheme for a component within its parent's layout flow.
TextAlign
Standard Library / Enums
Horizontal alignment of inline text content.
Options
Standard Library / Platform Types
sngl
android / Components
Composable
android / Types
Composable describes a composable lambda to emit for a Compose parameter that expects @Composable () -> Unit.
ComposableProp
android / Types
ComposableProp is a key-value pair for composable lambda parameters.
Options
android / Platform Types

fyne / Components
VariadicChildren
fyne / Types
VariadicChildren is a sentinel: when present in constructorArgs, the codegen renders all child nodes into a []fyne.CanvasObject slice and spreads them.
Options

SNGL
Docs
A purpose-built language for reactive, cross-platform UIs
Installation
Learn
How to install the SNGL CLI
Learn
Learn
Tutorials and guides for getting going with SNGL
Reference
Reference
Authoritative references for the SNGL language, standard library, and built-in targets
Language Specification
Reference
Formal grammar and semantics of the SNGL language
Effective SNGL
Reference
Comprehensive guide to writing idiomatic SNGL
android
Reference / Platforms
Android app. Emits Jetpack Compose; can mix in Go via gomobile when --lang go is used.
bubbletea
Reference / Platforms
Terminal UI, written in Go using the Bubble Tea framework.
fyne
Reference / Platforms
Cross-platform desktop GUI, written in Go using Fyne.
html
Reference / Platforms
Web output. Static site by default, or a language-driven HTTP server when paired with a language that implements HTTPCompiler.
none
Reference / Platforms
Marker platform for headless test execution — generates no code.
go
Reference / Languages
Generate Go source. Supports HTTP route mode and WASM bindings.
js
Reference / Languages
Generate JavaScript source.
kotlin
Reference / Languages
Generate Kotlin source (used with the Android platform).
none
Reference / Languages
Sentinel language for static output modes that need no backing code.

Effective SNGL

Introduction

SNGL is a declarative UI language that compiles to native code across multiple platforms -- HTML/JS, Go TUI (Bubbletea), Android (Compose), and desktop (Fyne). You write UI once in .sngl files; the compiler generates reactive, platform-specific output with no runtime overhead.

File Structure

Every .sngl file is a flat list of top-level declarations. Order does not matter -- the compiler resolves references in two passes.

The output block declares which language and platform combinations the file targets:


output {
    js { html }
    go { bubbletea(package="main") }
}

Imports pull in definitions from other .sngl files by directory path, or from host-language packages via scheme imports:

import "shared"

component main {
    var w shared.Widget
    text(value=w.name)
}

Scheme imports pull in types from host-language packages:

import "go://go/ast"

component main {
    var file ast.File
    text(value=string(file))
}

The top-level declarations available are: import, output, struct, enum, unit, style, const, var, func, components (PascalCase names), timer, and test.

A complete minimal file needs only a component main:

component main {
}

A realistic minimal file looks like this:

output {
    js { html }
}

component main {
    text(value="Hello, SNGL")
}

Every package that produces an executable must have exactly one component main. Packages without component main are libraries -- they export structs, enums, and components for other files to import.

Types

Primitives

bool, int, float, string work as expected. dyn is the escape hatch for untyped values -- use it for host interop, not as a default.

Special types

color, date, time, dateTime, duration, measurement, url, email, uuid, regex are built-in types with validation. Color has literal syntax (#ff0000). Duration and measurement have unit literal syntax (5s, 12px).

Regex

The regex type represents a compiled regular expression. Declare one with the regex() constructor or as a typed variable:

const pattern = regex("[a-z]+")
const emailPat regex = "^[^@]+@[^@]+$"

Invalid patterns are caught at compile time. Use regex.matches to check for a match and regex.find to extract the first match:

const pattern = regex("[a-z]+")
func _a() => pattern.matches("hello")
func _b() => pattern.find("abc 123")

Collections

list<T> is an ordered, typed collection:

var names list<string> = []
var scores = [100, 95, 87]

Optional types

option<T> wraps a value of type T that may be null. null is the default value for option types and is only valid for option types (not bare structs or primitives):

var name option<string>
var count option<int> = 5
var todo option<Todo> = null

Check for presence with == null / != null. Concrete values of T are implicitly assignable to option<T>.

Structs

Structs are value types. They cannot be null. Fields have zero-value defaults when not specified:

struct Todo {
    text string = ""
    done bool = false
}

var todo Todo
var todo2 = Todo{text="Buy eggs", done=false}

Accessing and mutating fields uses dot notation: todo.text, todo.done = true.

Enums

Named enums declare a fixed set of string values:

enum Status { active, inactive, pending }

Inline enums skip the top-level declaration when you need a one-off constraint:

var mode enum { light, dark } = "light"

At runtime, enum values are strings. The compiler validates assignments against declared variants.

Unit types

Units declare named suffixes with optional conversion factors:

unit duration { ms, s = 1000msms, m = 60ss, h = 60mm }
unit measurement { px, em, rem = 16emem, vw, vh, pct }

Same-base additions normalize automatically (1s + 500ms becomes 1500ms). Different-base additions produce compound values (16px + 2em). Scalar multiplication and division work as expected (3px * 2 is 6px).

Type inference

Types are inferred from initializers. Explicit types are needed when the initializer is ambiguous or absent:

var count = 0
var name = "World"
var bg = #ff0000
var timeout duration = "5s"
var todos list<Todo> = []
var status Status = "active"

The rule: if the right side is an empty list, a zero-value struct, an enum string, or a unit literal (which could match multiple unit types), annotate the type. Otherwise, let inference do its job.

Function types

Function types use func(ParamTypes) ReturnType syntax. Omit the return type for void:

var handler func() = null
var transform func(string) string = null
var callback func(string) int = null

Type Conversions

Explicit conversions

int(x), float(x), string(x), and bool(x) are the only cast forms. They accept primitive operands only — struct, func, component, list, and option values are rejected. The cast syntax never dispatches to user-defined methods; define a method and call it as x.string() instead.

func _a() => string(42)
func _b() => string(3.14)
func _c() => string(true)
func _d() => int("42")
func _e() => int(3.14)
func _f() => float(42)
func _g() => float("3.14")

Allow-lists per target:

Target Accepts
int int, float, string, bool, enum, unit
float int, float, string, bool, enum, unit
string any primitive / string-domain (color, date, …) / enum / unit
bool bool, string

Implicit conversions

Narrow list — most type changes are rejected and require an explicit cast.

From To When
int float Anywhere a float is expected (auto-promotion)
int literal 0 any unit type Typed zero: var t duration = 0, delay(0)
string string-domain Assignment: var d date = "2024-01-15" (validated)
string-domain string Assignment: var s string = myDate
string literal enum Assignment: var s Status = "active" (validated)
T option<T> Assignment: var x option<int> = 5
null option<T> Assignment: var x option<int> = null
null func(...) Compiles to a zero-value-returning callable stub
func() T T Zero-arg function auto-called where T is expected
any dyn dyn accepts any type
dyn any Escape hatch; no runtime check

float → int is not implicit — write int(x) to discard the fractional part. Non-zero int → unit is not implicit — use unit literals (5s) or multiply (n * 1s).

Disallowed conversions

These are compile errors:

Conversion Error
null → struct null is not assignable to struct type
struct → int() / float() / string() / bool() cannot convert — define a method instead
list/option/func/component → cast cannot convert — define a method instead
non-zero int → unit cannot initialize / cannot pass
wrong type → component param does not match
bad string literal → string-domain format-specific error (invalid date, invalid email)
wrong variant → enum is not a valid variant

String interpolation

Inside "{expr}", a primitive, string-domain type, enum, unit, null, list, option, or zero-arg function flows through automatically. For any other type — struct, component, etc. — the compiler looks up a .string() method on the value's type and calls it. When no such method exists the interpolation is a compile error:

struct Point {
    x int = 0
    y int = 0
}
func Point.string(p Point) => "({p.x},{p.y})"
var count = 42
var active = true
var origin = Point{x = 0, y = 0}
func label() => "count={count}, active={active}, origin={origin}"

Removing the Point.string method makes the {origin} interpolation a compile error, not a silent {...} render.

State

var -- mutable reactive state

var declares mutable state that triggers UI updates when changed. Group related vars:

var (
    count = 0
    name = "World"
    active = true
)

const -- compile-time values

const declares immutable values evaluated at compile time. Use them for configuration and magic numbers:

const (
    MAX_ITEMS = 100
    DEFAULT_NAME = "unnamed"
    PI float = 3.14159
)
const SINGLE = 42

Constants cannot reference var values.

Derived state with zero-arg functions

Zero-arg functions serve as derived state -- they auto-update reactively and are read-only:

var count = 0
func doubled() => count * 2
func label() => "Count: {count}"

Zero-arg functions are auto-invoked when referenced without (): text(value=label) calls label() implicitly.

Data events

Data events let you react to variable changes with inline statement blocks:

var todos list<Todo> @change {
    saveTodos(todos)
}
var count = 0 @init {
    loadCount()
}

Available events: @change (value changed), @init (component initialized), @insert(item) and @delete(item) (list-specific).

Gotchas

Structs cannot be null (var todo Todo = null is a compile error; use var todo Todo or var todo option<Todo> = null for nullable). Empty lists need an explicit type (var items list<string> = []) because the compiler cannot infer T from [].

Functions

Expression form

For single-expression pure functions, the body follows the parameter list directly:

func add(a int, b int) => a + b
func greet(name string) => "Hello, {name}!"

Block form

For multi-step logic, use a body with return:

func clamp(val int, lo int, hi int) int {
    var clamped = val < lo ? lo : val
    var result = clamped > hi ? hi : clamped
    return result
}

Void/action functions

Functions with no return type are void. They can mutate component state and are called from event handlers:

var count = 0
func reset() {
    count = 0
}
func increment(n int) {
    count += n
}

Void functions can only appear inside a component block. Pure functions (with a return type) can appear at the top level or inside components.

Type methods

Attach a function to a type with a dotted name. The first parameter is the receiver:

func int.double(x int) => x * 2
func string.shout(s string) => "{s}!"

Call with either syntax:

func _a() => int.double(5)
func _b() => 5.double()
func _c() => "hello".shout()

Type methods work on primitives (int, float, string, bool, color, list) and user-defined structs.

Generic functions

Type parameters go after the function name inside angle brackets. All stdlib list functions use generics:

func list.push<T>(l list<T>, item T) list<T> { ... }
func list.reverse<T>(l list<T>) list<T> { ... }
func list.indexOf<T>(l list<T>, item T) { ... }

Type parameters are inferred at call sites -- you never write <T> explicitly when calling a generic function:

var todos = [Todo{text="a", done=true}, Todo{text="b", done=false}]
func active() => list.filter(todos, (t) => !t.done)
func labels() => list.map(todos, (t) => t.text)

Lambdas

Inline functions for filtering and mapping:

var todos = [Todo{text="a", done=true}, Todo{text="b", done=false}]
func active() => list.filter(todos, (t) => !t.done)
func labels() => list.map(todos, (t) => t.text)

Lambda parameter types are inferred from context.

Gotchas

Pure functions cannot mutate state. Void functions cannot return values. Attempting either is a compile error.

Components

Declaration

A component groups params, state, functions, and visual nodes:

component Counter(label = "", start = 0) {
    var count = start
    hbox {
        text(value="{label}: {count}")
        button(text="+", @click { count += 1 })
    }
}

component main

component main is the app entry point. Its body becomes the root of the rendered UI. Every executable file needs exactly one.

Params

Params are the component's public API. They are declared in parentheses after the component name and accept values from parent components:

component MyWidget(label = "default", count int, size enum { small, medium, large } = "medium") {
    text(value=label)
}

Without a default, params use the type's zero value. Parents pass params as named arguments:

Counter(label="Clicks", start=10)
Counter()

Composition

Components can use other components:

Counter(label="Score")

Imported components are namespaced by their import path (e.g., widgets.Counter(label="Imported")).

Stdlib components

The standard library provides layout (vbox, hbox, stack, spacer), input (input, checkbox, toggle, select, textarea, radio), display (text, image, badge, progress, spinner), navigation (tabs, link), and overlay (modal, drawer, tooltip, popover) components.

Visual Nodes

Syntax

Visual nodes are component instances rendered as UI elements:

text(value="hello", style={fontSize = 24})

Both () and {} are optional:

spacer
text(value="Hello")
vbox { text(value="Hi") }

Params

Params are typed values defined by the component's declaration. The compiler validates param names and types:

button(text="Submit", disabled=count <= 0)
input(value=name, placeholder="Enter name", type="email")
image(src="photo.png", alt="Profile", fit="cover")

Events

Events use the @ prefix and contain mutation statements:

var count = 0
button(text="+", @click { count += 1 })
input(value=name, @input { name = event.value })

Multi-statement events separate with semicolons:

var (
    newTodo = ""
    todos list<Todo> = []
)
button(text="Add", @click {
    todos.push(Todo{text = newTodo, done = false})
    newTodo = ""
})

Available events depend on the component (check the stdlib definition). Use event.value to access the event payload in input handlers.

Inline styles

Apply styles directly on any node:

vbox(style={padding = 16, gap = 12})
text(value="hello", style={color = #007700, fontSize = 24, fontWeight = "bold"})

Conditional rendering

Use if blocks. if does not support else -- use two if blocks with opposite conditions (note: else is only available on for loops):

var active = true
if active {
    text(value="Active", style={color = #007700})
}
if !active {
    text(value="Inactive", style={color = #CC0000})
}

Iteration

var items = ["a", "b", "c"]
for item = items {
    text(value=item)
}
for item, idx = items {
    text(value="{idx}: {item}", key=idx)
}

Use key for stable identity across re-renders when the list changes.

for...else

The else block renders when the list is empty. It works with both for item = list and for item, index = list:

var items list<string> = []
for item = items {
    text(value=item)
} else {
    text(value="No items yet")
}

Element refs

Tag a node with #id to reference it in tests:

var count = 0
button.#inc(text="+", @click { count += 1 })
text.#display(value="Count: {count}")

Events and Mutations

Assignment operators

=, +=, -=, *=, /=, %= work on vars and struct fields:

var count = 0
var label = ""
button(text="+5", @click { count += 5 })
button(text="tag", @click { label += " tagged" })

Toggle

!! flips a boolean in place:

var active = true
button(text="Toggle", @click { active!! })

List mutations

Method syntax mutates in place. Function syntax returns a new list:

var items = [1, 2, 3]
button(text="Add", @click { items.push(4) })
button(text="Remove first", @click { items.remove(0) })

Multiple statements

Separate statements with semicolons inside event handlers:

var (
    a = 0
    b = 0
)
button(text="go", @click {
    a += 1
    b += 2
})

Emit

Fire a component event to notify the parent:

var data = "saved"
button(text="Save", @click { @save(data) })

Gotchas

Mutations are only allowed in event handlers and void functions. You cannot mutate state inside a pure function.

Timers

component main {
    var (
        progress float = 0
        running = true
    )
    timer(interval=100msms, enabled=running, @tick {
        progress += 0.1
    })
    text(value=string(progress))
    button(text="Stop", @click { running = false })
}

func testTimerIncrements(t Test, c main) {
    t.assert(c.progress == 0)
    t.tick()
    t.assert(c.progress == 0.1)
}

The first argument is a duration literal. The second is a bool var controlling start/stop. The body executes on each tick while the bool is true. In tests, tick() advances one interval.

Gotchas

Timer bodies can mutate state, like event handlers. The timer does not fire if the controlling bool is false, even if you call tick() in a test.

Style

Inline styles

All styles are applied via the style param:

vbox(style={gap = 12, padding = 16})
text(value="bold", style={fontWeight = "bold", color = #007700, fontSize = 24})
button(text="go", style={margin = 4, background = #ff0000, padding = 8})

Named styles

Declare reusable styles at the top level and apply them with class:

style
primary {
    color = #0000ff
    fontWeight = "bold"
    fontSize = 16
}

style
secondary {
    color = #777777
    fontStyle = "italic"
}

component main {
    vbox {
        text(value="hello", class="primary")
        text(value="world", class="secondary")
    }
}

Colors

Hex literals with optional alpha: #ff0000, #fff, #00000080. Use color.rgb() and color.rgba() for dynamic construction. Access channels with .r, .g, .b, .a.

Units in styles

12px, 1.5em, 16rem. A bare number uses the platform default unit.

Gotchas

Style property names are camelCase: fontSize, fontWeight, borderRadius. Not font-size, not font_size.

Testing

Test blocks

Tests target a specific component and get a fresh copy of its state:

component counter {
    var count = 0

    button #inc(text="+", @click { count += 1 })
    text #display(value="Count: {count}")
}

func testStartsAtZero(t Test, c counter) {
    t.assert(c.count == 0)
}

func testIncrements(t Test, c counter) {
    c.#inc.@click()
    t.assert(c.count == 1)
    t.assert(c.#display.value == "Count: 1")
}

assert

assert(condition) fails the test with a diagnostic if the condition is false.

Element refs

Tag nodes with #id, then access props and fire events in tests:

var count = 0
button.#inc(text="+", @click { count += 1 })
text.#display(value="Count: {count}")

In a for loop, refs become indexed: #item[0].value, #item[2].value.

Access props: #display.value, #display.class. Fire events: #inc.@click(). Check existence: #conditional != null.

State isolation

Each test block starts with fresh state. Mutations in one test do not leak to another.

tick

tick() advances all active timers by one interval. See the Timers section for a complete example.

Nested tests

Subtests inherit parent state but get their own snapshot. Changes in the subtest do not affect the outer scope:

component app {
    var x = 0
    text(value="{x}")
}

func testNesting(t Test, c app) {
    c.x = 1
    t.test("inner")
    t.assert(c.x == 1)
}

Disabling tests

Prefix with /- to comment out a test block: /-test app "skipped" { assert(false) }.

Tests without descriptions

A test block can omit the description string: test app { assert(count == 0) }.

Platform Targets

The output block (see File Structure) determines compilation targets. Override from the CLI: sngl compile --lang go --platform bubbletea. Other commands: sngl run (compile and execute), sngl build (distributable artifact), sngl test (run all test blocks).

Each platform maps stdlib components to native widgets. Core components (vbox, hbox, text, button, input, checkbox) work everywhere. Overlay components (modal, drawer, popover) have varying support.

Naming Conventions

Category Convention Examples
Files kebab-case.sngl todo-item.sngl
Components PascalCase TodoItem, Counter
Variables/params camelCase newTodo, isActive
Structs/enums PascalCase Todo, Status
Style properties camelCase fontSize, fontWeight
Events @camelCase @click, @longPress
Constants camelCase or UPPER_CASE defaultName, MAX_ITEMS

Common Patterns

Todo list

struct Todo {
    text string = ""
    done bool = false
}

component main {
    var (
        newTodo = ""
        todos list<Todo> = []
    )
    vbox(style={padding = 16, gap = 8}) {
        hbox(style={gap = 8}) {
            input(value=newTodo, placeholder="New todo", @input { newTodo = event.value })
            button(text="Add", disabled=newTodo == "", @click {
                todos.push(Todo{text = newTodo, done = false})
                newTodo = ""
            })
        }
        for todo, idx = todos {
            hbox(key=idx, style={gap = 8}) {
                checkbox(checked=todo.done, @change { todos[idx].done!! })
                text(value=todo.text)
                button(text="x", @click { todos.remove(idx) })
            }
        }
    }
}

Form with validation

component main {
    var email = ""
    func valid() => string.contains(email, "@") && string.length(email) > 3
    vbox(style={padding = 16, gap = 8}) {
        input(value=email, placeholder="Email", type="email", @input { email = event.value })
        if !valid {
            text(value="Enter a valid email", style={color = #CC0000, fontSize = 12})
        }
        button(text="Submit", disabled=!valid)
    }
}

Modal open/close

component main {
    var showModal = false
    vbox(style={padding = 16}) {
        button(text="Open", @click { showModal = true })
        modal(open=showModal, title="Settings", @close { showModal = false }) {
            text(value="Content goes here")
            button(text="Close", @click { showModal = false })
        }
    }
}

Timer animation

component main {
    var (
        progress float = 0.0
        running = false
    )
    timer(interval=50msms, enabled=running, @tick {
        progress += 0.01
    })
    vbox(style={padding = 16, gap = 8}) {
        progress(value=progress, max=1.0, showValue=true)
        button(text=running ? "Pause" : "Start", @click { running!! })
        button(text="Reset", @click {
            progress = 0.0
            running = false
        })
    }
}

Filtered list

struct Todo {
    text string = ""
    done bool = false
}

component main {
    var todos = [Todo{text="Write docs", done=true}, Todo{text="Fix bug", done=false}]
    func active() => list.filter(todos, (t) => !t.done)
    func activeCount() => list.length(active)

    vbox(style={padding=16, gap=8}) {
        text(value="{activeCount} remaining")
        for todo, idx = todos {
            checkbox(key=idx, checked=todo.done, label=todo.text, @change { todos[idx].done!! })
        }
    }
}
